name: Southern & SE Asian Scripts
description: Master the art of distinguishing between the rounded, curly, and ornate scripts of South and Southeast Asia.

questions.explanation: Extremely dense and circular; lacks the top 'ticks' seen in Telugu or Kannada.
Distinctly 'coiled' look. It lacks the horizontal lines of Hindi and is more 'bubbly' than Tamil.
Look for the distinct 'check-mark' or 'tick' (talakattu) at the top of the characters.
Virtually identical to Telugu but often features slightly more complex internal loops.
Defined by the large 'umbrella' or curved cap over almost every letter.
The 'roundest' script. Composed almost entirely of overlapping circles.
Notice the small 'heads' (tiny circles) at the start of the strokes and its vertical, fluid look.
Similar to Thai but more rounded/loopy with fewer sharp angles.

questions.text: តើអ្នកសុខសប្បាយជាទេ?
questions.explanation: Highly ornate and jagged. It looks heavier and more 'ancient' than Thai.
A traditional script with a very wavy, consistent 'mountain-peak' flow.
The word for 'Thanks'. Very circular and lacks top-side adornments.
Note the intricate 'y' shapes at the end which are unique to Sri Lankan Sinhala.
The check-marks are clearly visible on the first and third characters.
Note the blockier nature of some loops compared to Telugu.
The 'umbrella' curves are the dead giveaway here.

questions.text: ကျေးဇူးတင်ပါတယ်။
questions.explanation: Pure circularity. No straight lines or sharp corners.
The small loops at the base of the letters are characteristic of Thai.
More simplified and rounded than its Thai counterpart.
Deeply intricate with small 'tails' on top of characters.
Consistent zigzag/wavy pattern across the baseline.
The 'm' sound at the end is a small circle, common in Malayalam.
The famous Sri Lankan greeting; note the 'coiled' aesthetic.
Similar to Malayalam but uses the top tick mark.
Features the top tick but lacks the final circular 'm' found in Telugu/Malayalam versions.
The first character is encased in the signature Odia round cap.
Looks like a series of interconnected zeros or 'o's.

questions.text: ยินดีต้อนรับ
questions.explanation: Features many vertical stalks with small 'heads'.

questions.text: ຍິນດີຕ້ອນຮັບ
questions.explanation: Notice the softer, more cursive flow compared to Thai.

questions.text: សូមស្វាគមន៍
questions.explanation: The most complex and 'busy' looking script in this group.
The script looks like a continuous wave across the page.
No top ticks; extremely compact and round.
The vowel markers on the left of characters are very 'curly'.
Classic Telugu structure with the 'check' marks on top.
Round, but the 'tick' is slightly more angled than Telugu.
The 'umbrella' shape is dominant throughout.
Very little variation in stroke height; mostly circles.
The first character is a tall, thin vowel marker with a head at the bottom.
The 'u' sound marker (bottom) is very loopy.
Distinctive 'roofs' on characters that are more jagged than Odia.
Distinctive tall markers at the start and end of segments.
Round, but contains the 'ksh' conjunct which is very dense.
The left-side vowel marker is a single, large loop.
Notice the 'm' (circle with a tick) at the end.
Lacks the final circular 'm' used in Telugu for this word.
The final character has a unique 'y' hook under the umbrella.
The stacking of circles is very common in Burmese words.
Relatively few 'heads' in this specific word, but very vertical.
The 'n' at the end (last char) is more curved than Thai.
The first letter has a double-leg look common in Khmer.
Consistent rhythm and height across the word.
The word for 'water'. Very loopy and finishes with a circle.
Each character is almost a self-contained coil.
The long 'ii' vowel marker is a curl on top of the first letter.
Similar to Telugu, but the 'u' marker on the second letter is distinct.
Two very prominent round caps.
Only two characters, both based on the circle.
The small circle above the second character is a vowel marker.
Similar to Thai but with more 'swooping' lines.
The 'k' at the end is very jagged and ornate.
A very short, wavy example of the script.
